session.user:
⎿ https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/pull/8719 (OPEN)
⏺ Bash(erk exec impl-signal submitted 2>/dev/null || true) ⎿ { "success": false, "event": "submitted", … +3 lines (ctrl+o to expand)
⏺ Bash(erk pr check --stage=impl 2>&1 || true) ⎿ Checking PR #8719 for branch plnd/closure-capture-exception-03-04-0818...
[PASS] .erk/impl-context/ not present (cleaned up) … +3 lines (ctrl+o to expand)
⏺ Plan execution complete.
Summary: - Added "Loop closure capture" as the 4th acceptable exception to the no-default-parameters rule in .claude/skills/dignified-python/references/api-design.md - Verified label_ops.py already has the correct def attempt(label: str = label) pattern — no changes needed - All CI checks pass (5551+ tests) - PR: https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/pull/8719
✻ Baked for 3m 45s
❯ /local:statusline-refresh ⎿ 🔄 Status line refreshed
❯ /local:debug-ci
⏺ Bash(git branch --show-current) ⎿ plnd/closure-capture-exception-03-04-0818
⏺ Bash(gh run list --branch plnd/closure-capture-exception-03-04-0818 --json conclusion,name,databaseId,status --jq '.[] | select(.conclusion == "failure") | "\(.datab…) ⎿ (No output)
⏺ All CI checks are passing — no failures found for this branch.
❯ /local:statusline-refresh ⎿ 🔄 Status line refreshed
❯ why does statusbar look like this ⎿ [Image #1]
⏺ The status bar shows:
- st:👀 — PR is in review state - chks:[✅:15 🚫:1] — 15 checks passing, 1 check blocked/failing
The 🚫:1 means one check has a non-passing status. The debug-ci command only looked for conclusion == "failure" — this check might have a different conclusion like "action_required" or "cancelled".
Let me check what that one check is.
⏺ Bash(gh run list --branch plnd/closure-capture-exception-03-04-0818 --json conclusion,name,databaseId,status --jq '.[] | select(.conclusion != "success" and .conclus…) ⎿ failed to parse jq expression (line 1, column 26) .[] | select(.conclusion \!= "success" and .conclusion \!= "") | "\(.conclusion) \(.name) \(.databaseId)" ^ unexpected token "\\"
⏺ Bash(gh pr checks 8719 2>&1 | head -20) ⎿ check-submission pass 5s https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/actions/runs/22678499275/job/65741939607 discover pass 13s https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/actions/runs/22678499238/job/65741937581 erk-mcp-tests pass 18s https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/actions/runs/22678499275/job/65742103851 … +13 lines (ctrl+o to expand)
⏺ Found it — docs-check is failing. This is because we modified the skill file and the docs sync check detected it's out of date. Let me check the logs.
⏺ Bash(gh run view 22678499275 --log-failed 2>&1 | tail -30) ⎿ (No output)
⏺ Bash(gh run view 22678499275 --log-failed 2>&1 | head -60) ⎿ (No output)
⏺ Bash(gh api repos/dagster-io/erk/actions/runs/22678499275/jobs --jq '.jobs[] | select(.name == "docs-check") | .steps[] | select(.conclusion != "success") | "\(.name…) ⎿ Error: Exit code 1 failed to parse jq expression (line 1, column 73) .jobs[] | select(.name == "docs-check") | .steps[] | select(.conclusion \!= "success") | "\(.name): \(.conclusion)" ^ unexpected token "\\"
failed to parse jq expression (line 1, column 73) .jobs[] | select(.name == "docs-check") | .steps[] | select(.conclusion \!= "success") | "\(.name): \(.conclusion)" ^ unexpected token "\\"
⏺ Bash(gh api repos/dagster-io/erk/actions/runs/22678499275/jobs --jq '.jobs[] | select(.name == "docs-check") | .steps[] | select(.conclusion == "failure") | "\(.name…) ⎿ (No output)
⏺ Bash(gh run view 22678499275 --job 65742103953 --log 2>&1 | tail -40) ⎿ docs-check Run /./.github/actions/setup-prettier 2026-03-04T16:25:57.6493386Z docs-check Run /./.github/actions/setup-prettier 2026-03-04T16:25:57.6502088Z added 1 package in 2m docs-check Run /./.github/actions/setup-prettier 2026-03-04T16:25:57.6502366Z … +30 lines (ctrl+o to expand)
⏺ The docs-check failure is not related to your code — it's a CI infrastructure issue. The job timed out trying to download cpython-3.13.12 (tried 3 times over ~15 minutes, then was cancelled). This is a transient GitHub Actions / uv cache miss.
Summary: Your code is fine. The 🚫:1 is docs-check failing due to a Python download timeout in CI, not a code issue. You can re-run the check or ignore it.
✻ Baked for 49s
how can we improve our ci pipeline to avoid this? seems like we shouldn't be reinstalling python everytime
session.thinking: The user wants to improve their CI pipeline to avoid repeatedly downloading Python, which caused a timeout. Let me explore the CI configuration to understand the current setup.

tool_use.name: Agent
tool_use.param: FIRST check docs/learned/index.md for existing documentation on CI topics. Read relevant d...[truncated 624 chars]...t back the full picture of how Python and dependencies are currently installed and cached.

session.tool_result:
Perfect! Now I have enough information to provide a comprehensive report. Let me compile the findings:
## Summary: How Python and Dependencies Are Currently Installed and Cached in Erk CI
Based on my exploration of the CI pipeline configuration, here's the complete picture:
### 1. **Python Installation Method**
**Current Approach:** - **Primary**: Custom composite action `.github/actions/setup-python-uv/action.yml` - **Alternative**: Direct `actions/setup-python@v5` (only in the autofix job)
The setup-python-uv action: ```yaml - uses: actions/setup-python@v5 with: python-version: ${{ inputs.python-version }} - name: Install uv shell: bash run: pip install uv - name: Install dependencies shell: bash run: uv sync --python ${{ inputs.python-version }} ```
**Versions Tested:** - The CI workflow tests Python 3.11, 3.12, 3.13, and 3.14 in the `unit-tests` job (via matrix strategy) - Default version: 3.12
### 2. **uv Configuration**
... [95 lines omitted] ...
session.thinking: Good, now I have a clear picture. Let me look at the specific files to understand the current setup and design a plan.
session.assistant: Let me read the key files to design the improvement.
session.tool_result: 1→name: "Setup Python with uv" 2→description: "Install Python, uv, and sync dependencies" 3→ 4→inputs: 5→ python-version: 6→ description: "Python version to use" 7→ required: false 8→ default: "3.12" 9→ 10→runs: 11→ using: "composite" 12→ steps: 13→ - uses: actions/setup-python@v5 14→ with: 15→ python-version: ${{ inputs.python-version }} 16→ - name: Install uv 17→ shell: bash 18→ run: pip install uv 19→ - name: Install dependencies 20→ shell: bash 21→ run: uv sync --python ${{ inputs.python-version }} 22→

1→--- 2→title: Container-less CI with Native Tool Installation 3→read_when: 4→ - Setting up Claude Code in GitHub Actions without containers 5→ - Comparing container vs container-less CI approaches 6→ - Choosing between container and container-less CI approaches 7→last_audited: "2026-02-05 20:38 PT" 8→audit_result: edited 9→--- 10→ 11→# Container-less CI with Native Tool Installation 12→ 13→## Overview 14→ 15→This document covers the container-less approach for running Claude Code in GitHub Actions. For the container-based alternative, see [claude-code-docker.md](claude-code-docker.md). 16→ 17→**Pros:** No image maintenance, simpler workflow, no permission workarounds needed. 18→ 19→**Cons:** Network dependency on first run (binary is cached afterward), potential version drift. 20→ 21→## Implementation: Composite Actions 22→ 23→All erk workflows use composite actions to consolidate setup. There are two layers: 24→ 25→1. **`.github/actions/setup-claude-code/action.yml`** -- Downloads the Claude Code binary directly from GCS (bypassing the install script, which hangs in CI). Includes `actions/cache` so the binary is only downloaded on cache miss. Installs to `~/.local/bin/claude`. 26→ 27→2. **`.github/actions/erk-remote-setup/action.yml`** -- The full setup action used by most workflows. Calls `setup-claude-code`, installs uv (via `astral-sh/setup-uv@v5`), installs prettier, installs erk, validates Claude credentials, and configures git identity. 28→ 29→### Why Direct Download Instead of Install Script 30→
... [62 lines omitted] ...
81→### Permission denied errors

1→--- 2→title: Composite Action Patterns 3→read_when: 4→ - creating reusable GitHub Actions setup steps 5→ - using erk-remote-setup composite action 6→ - understanding GitHub Actions composite patterns 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "Use direct GCS download for Claude Code installation" 9→ warning: "NEVER use the curl | bash install script for Claude Code in CI — it hangs unpredictably. Use direct GCS download via setup-claude-code action." 10→ - action: "Use erk-remote-setup for consolidated secret validation" 11→ warning: "NEVER duplicate secret validation across workflows — use erk-remote-setup's consolidated validation." 12→ - action: "Include cache keys for downloaded binaries" 13→ warning: "NEVER skip cache keys for downloaded binaries — cache saves 10-20s per workflow run." 14→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 15→audit_result: clean 16→--- 17→ 18→# Composite Action Patterns 19→ 20→GitHub Actions composite actions encapsulate reusable setup steps. Erk uses them to avoid duplicating 7-step setup sequences across remote AI workflows. 21→ 22→## Available Composite Actions 23→ 24→| Action | Purpose | Inputs | 25→| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- | 26→| `erk-remote-setup` | Full remote workflow environment setup | `erk-pat`, `anthropic-api-key`, `claude-code-oauth-token` | 27→| `setup-claude-code` | Install Claude Code CLI with caching | None | 28→| `setup-python-uv` | Install Python and uv, sync dependencies | `python-version` (default: "3.12") | 29→| `setup-graphite` | Install Graphite CLI for stack management | None | 30→| `setup-claude-erk` | Install erk tools (assumes uv/claude exist) | None |
... [228 lines omitted] ...
48→Standard GitHub Actions workflow errors are opaque: "Secret not found" doesn't tell you which secret or which step failed. 50→**Pattern**: Validate all required secrets in the first composite action step with explicit error titles. 57→ echo "::error title=Missing Secret::API_KEY not configured" 67→- **Clear error titles** in GitHub UI (visible without expanding logs) 223→3. **Can this fail independently?** → If yes, separate action for better error messages 245→GitHub Actions doesn't validate composite action dependencies. Undocumented dependencies cause silent failures when actions are reused in new contexts.
session.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture. The issue is:
1. `setup-python-uv` uses `actions/setup-python@v5` which downloads Python from GitHub's tool cache (or from python.org if not cached) 2. The `docs-check` job timed out downloading cpython-3.13.12 — this means the Python version wasn't in GitHub's runner tool cache 3. There's no uv cache in `setup-python-uv`
The fix is straightforward: switch `setup-python-uv` to use `astral-sh/setup-uv@v5` which:
- Has built-in dependency caching - Manages Python installation through uv (which has its own cache)
This approach is better than just enabling cache on `actions/setup-python@v5` because it aligns with what the remote workflows already do, giving us consistent uv caching and faster Python installation. Now I need to check which jobs in the CI workflow are using `setup-python-uv` so I can update them.
session.tool_result:

... [452 lines omitted] ...
84→ echo "::error::Files need formatting or docs are out of sync" 93→ echo "::error::Formatting issues found (cannot auto-fix fork PRs)" 196→ (needs.format.result == 'failure' || 197→ needs.lint.result == 'failure' || 198→ needs.fix-formatting.result == 'failure' || 199→ needs.docs-check.result == 'failure' || 200→ needs.ty.result == 'failure') 313→ # Inlined from .github/actions/setup-claude-erk to avoid CodeQL warning 340→ - name: Collect failure context 342→ id: failures 353→ - name: Collect actual errors 355→ id: errors 357→ errors="" 359→ if [ "${{ needs.format.result }}" = "failure" ]; then 360→ errors+="=== RUFF FORMAT ERRORS ===
" 361→ errors+="$(uv run ruff format --check . 2>&1 || true)

" 364→ if [ "${{ needs.lint.result }}" = "failure" ]; then 365→ errors+="=== RUFF LINT ERRORS ===
" 366→ errors+="$(uv run ruff check . 2>&1 || true)

" 369→ if [ "${{ needs.fix-formatting.result }}" = "failure" ]; then 370→ errors+="=== MARKDOWN-FIX ERRORS ===
" 371→ errors+="$(prettier --check '**/*.md' --ignore-path .gitignore 2>&1 || true)

" 374→ if [ "${{ needs.docs-check.result }}" = "failure" ]; then 375→ errors+="=== DOCS CHECK ERRORS ===
" 376→ errors+="$(make md-check 2>&1 || true)
" 377→ errors+="$(make docs-check 2>&1 || true)

" 380→ if [ "${{ needs.ty.result }}" = "failure" ]; then 381→ errors+="=== TY TYPE ERRORS ===
" 382→ errors+="$(uv run ty check 2>&1 || true)

" 386→ echo -e "$errors" > /tmp/errors.txt 397→ echo "Debug: erk version: $(erk --version 2>&1 || echo 'version failed')" 407→ --var-file "ERRORS=/tmp/errors.txt" 2>&1); then 408→ echo "Error: Failed to get ci-autofix prompt" 415→ echo "Error: ci-autofix prompt is empty (command succeeded but no output)" 437→ # === CI Failure Summarization (Haiku) === 456→ (needs.format.result == 'failure' || 457→ needs.lint.result == 'failure' || 458→ needs.fix-formatting.result == 'failure' || 459→ needs.docs-check.result == 'failure' || 460→ needs.ty.result == 'failure' || 461→ needs.unit-tests.result == 'failure' || 462→ needs.integration-tests.result == 'failure' || 463→ needs.erk-mcp-tests.result == 'failure') 477→ - name: Summarize failures

1→name: docs 2→ 3→on: 4→ push: 5→ branches: [master] 6→ paths: 7→ - "docs/**" 8→ - "mkdocs.yml" 9→ workflow_dispatch: 10→ 11→permissions: 12→ contents: write 13→ 14→jobs: 15→ deploy: 16→ runs-on: ubuntu-latest 17→ timeout-minutes: 15 18→ steps: 19→ - uses: actions/checkout@v4 20→ - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-python-uv 21→ - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages 22→ run: uv run mkdocs gh-deploy --force 23→

1→name: "Setup Erk Remote Environment" 2→description: "Consolidated setup for remote workflow execution" 3→ 4→inputs: 5→ erk-pat: 6→ description: "ERK_QUEUE_GH_PAT secret" 7→ required: true 8→ anthropic-api-key: 9→ description: "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY secret" 10→ required: false 11→ claude-code-oauth-token: 12→ description: "CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN secret" 13→ required: false 14→ 15→runs: 16→ using: "composite" 17→ steps: 18→ - name: Validate secrets 19→ shell: bash 20→ run: | 21→ if [ -z "${{ inputs.erk-pat }}" ]; then 22→ echo "::error title=Missing Secret::ERK_QUEUE_GH_PAT not configured" 23→ exit 1 24→ fi 25→ if [ -z "${{ inputs.anthropic-api-key }}" ] && [ -z "${{ inputs.claude-code-oauth-token }}" ]; then 26→ echo "::error title=Missing Secret::Either ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN must be configured" 27→ exit 1 28→ fi 29→ 30→ - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5
... [31 lines omitted] ...
session.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture. The problem and solution are straightforward:
**Problem**: `setup-python-uv` uses `actions/setup-python@v5` + `pip install uv`. When a Python version isn't in GitHub's runner tool cache (like cpython-3.13.12), it downloads from python.org, which can timeout. No uv dependency caching either.
**Solution**: Replace `setup-python-uv` to use `astral-sh/setup-uv@v5` (like `erk-remote-setup` already does). Benefits: 1. `astral-sh/setup-uv` has built-in dependency caching
2. uv handles Python installation directly with its own caching layer, making it faster 3. This standardizes the approach across all CI jobs
Let me write the plan.
session.assistant: Now I have a clear picture. Let me write the plan.

tool_use.name: Write
tool_use.param:
# Plan: Switch CI Python setup to astral-sh/setup-uv
## Context
The `docs-check` CI job ...[truncated 2382 chars]... that the matrix Python versions (3.11, 3.12, 3.13, 3.14) all work with uv-managed Python

session.assistant:
Simple, targeted change — switch from `actions/setup-python@v5` + `pip install uv` to `astral-sh/setup-uv@v5` (which you already use in remote workflows). This gives you uv-managed Python downloads with built-in caching, eliminating the python.org download timeouts.
Two files to touch: the `setup-python-uv` action and the inlined autofix job setup.

tool_use.name: ExitPlanMode
tool_use.param: [{'tool': 'Bash', 'prompt': 'run git commands for committing and pushing'}]

# Plan: Switch CI Python setup to astral-sh/setup-uv
## Context
The `docs-check` CI job ...[truncated 2382 chars]... that the matrix Python versions (3.11, 3.12, 3.13, 3.14) all work with uv-managed Python

PreToolUse:ExitPlanMode hook error: [command -v erk >/dev/null 2>&1 || exit 0; ERK_HOOK_ID=exit-plan-mode-hook erk exec exit-plan-mode-hook]: DISPLAY PLAN: Before asking the question below, read the plan file and display its contents to the user with proper markdown formatting: /Users/schrockn/.claude/plans/zany-spinning-whistle.md
PLAN SAVE PROMPT
A plan exists for this session but has not been saved.
Use AskUserQuestion to ask the user: question: "📋 Plan: Switch CI Python setup to astral-sh/setup-uv Current context: (wt:erk) (br:master)\n\nWhat would you like to do with this plan?" header: "br:master"
IMPORTANT: Present options in this exact order: 1. "Create a plan PR on new branch" - Create a new branch, save plan as draft PR, and stop. Does NOT proceed to implementation. 2. "Just implement on the current branch without creating a PR." - Implement directly on the current branch without saving a plan PR. 3. "View/Edit the Plan" - Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.
⚠️ WARNING: Currently on 'master'. We strongly discourage editing directly on the trunk branch. Consider saving the plan and implementing in a dedicated worktree instead.
If user chooses 'Create a plan PR on new branch': 1. Run /erk:plan-save 2. STOP - Do NOT call ExitPlanMode. The plan-save command handles everything. Stay in plan mode and let the user exit manually if desired.
If user chooses 'Just implement on the current branch without creating a PR.': 1. Create implement-now marker: erk exec marker create --session-id 027e57aa-eb20-41fe-a4d3-129b0d6cf008 \ exit-plan-mode-hook.implement-now 2. Call ExitPlanMode 3. After exiting plan mode, implement the changes directly on the current branch.

This happens because `setup-python-uv` uses `actions/setup-python@v5`, which downloads Python from python.org when the version isn't pre-cached on the GitHub runner. Meanwhile, the remote workflows (`erk-remote-setup`) already use `astral-sh/setup-uv@v5`, which handles Python installation through uv with built-in caching. 6→ 7→## Problem 8→ 9→The `setup-python-uv` composite action: 10→1. Uses `actions/setup-python@v5` — downloads Python from python.org (can timeout) 11→2. Installs uv via `pip install uv` — slow, no caching 12→3. Has no uv dependency cache — re-downloads packages every run 13→ 14→## Solution 15→ 16→Replace `setup-python-uv` to use `astral-sh/setup-uv@v5`, which: 17→- Installs uv directly (no pip needed) 18→- Has built-in dependency caching by default 19→- Lets uv manage Python installation (uv downloads Python itself, with its own cache) 20→- Is already proven in `erk-remote-setup` 21→ 22→## Changes 23→ 24→### 1. Update `.github/actions/setup-python-uv/action.yml` 25→ 26→Replace the current implementation: 27→ 28→```yaml 29→# Before 30→- uses: actions/setup-python@v5
... [55 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant:
Here's the plan:
# Plan: Switch CI Python setup to astral-sh/setup-uv
## Context
The `docs-check` CI job failed with a 15-minute timeout trying to download `cpython-3.13.12` from python.org. This happens because `setup-python-uv` uses `actions/setup-python@v5`, which downloads Python from python.org when the version isn't pre-cached on the GitHub runner. Meanwhile, the remote workflows (`erk-remote-setup`) already use `astral-sh/setup-uv@v5`, which handles Python installation through uv with built-in caching.
## Problem
The `setup-python-uv` composite action: 1. Uses `actions/setup-python@v5` — downloads Python from python.org (can timeout) 2. Installs uv via `pip install uv` — slow, no caching 3. Has no uv dependency cache — re-downloads packages every run
## Solution
Replace `setup-python-uv` to use `astral-sh/setup-uv@v5`, which: - Installs uv directly (no pip needed) - Has built-in dependency caching by default - Lets uv manage Python installation (uv downloads Python itself, with its own cache) - Is already proven in `erk-remote-setup`
## Changes
**Two files**: `.github/actions/setup-python-uv/action.yml` and the inlined autofix job in `ci.yml`
## Verification
- Push to a branch and verify all CI jobs pass - Confirm faster setup times in CI logs - Check that matrix Python versions (3.11-3.14) all work with uv-managed Python

tool_use.name: AskUserQuestion
tool_use.param: [{'question': '📋 Plan: Switch CI Python setup to astral-sh/setup-uv\nCurrent context: (wt:...[truncated 432 chars]...ion': 'Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.'}], 'multiSelect': False}]

session.tool_result:
User has answered your questions: "📋 Plan: Switch CI Python setup to astral-sh/setup-uv Current context: (wt:erk) (br:master)
What would you like to do with this plan?"="Create a plan PR on new branch". You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.
session.user:
# /erk:plan-save
Save the current session's plan to GitHub with session context.
## Usage
```bash /erk:plan-save # Standalone plan /erk:plan-save --plan-type=learn # Learn plan (erk-learn label) /erk:plan-save --objective=123 # Explicit objective link (overrides marker) /erk:plan-save --current-branch # Use current branch instead of creating new plnd/ branch ```
Objective linking: if a plan was created via `/erk:objective-plan`, the session's objective-context marker is read automatically by the save command. Use `--objective=<number>` to override the marker or provide the link when no marker exists (e.g., during replan).
## Plan Storage
The plan is saved using the configured backend:
- **Draft PR backend** (`PLAN_BACKEND = "draft_pr"`): Creates a branch, pushes a plan commit, and opens a draft PR. Plan content is in the PR body after the metadata separator. The JSON output contract includes `plan_number`, `plan_url`, `title`, `branch_name`, `plan_backend`.
## Agent Instructions
### Step 1: Parse Arguments
Check `` for the `--plan-type` and `--objective` flags:
``` If contains "--plan-type=<type>": - Extract the type (standard or learn) - Store as PLAN_TYPE variable - Set PLAN_TYPE_FLAG to "--plan-type=<type>" Else: - Set PLAN_TYPE_FLAG to empty string
If contains "--objective=<number>": - Extract the number - Set OBJECTIVE_FLAG to "--objective=<number>" Else: - Set OBJECTIVE_FLAG to empty string ```
### Step 2: Generate Branch Slug
**Skip this step if `--current-branch` was passed.** The current branch is used directly.
Before saving, generate a branch slug from the plan title. Read the plan title from the plan file you wrote in plan mode (the first `# ` heading).
Generate a branch slug from the title:
- 2-4 hyphenated lowercase words, max 30 characters - Capture distinctive essence, drop filler words (the, a, for, implementation, plan) - Prefer action verbs: add, fix, refactor, update, consolidate, extract, migrate - Examples: "fix-auth-session", "add-plan-validation", "refactor-gateway-abc"
Store the result as `BRANCH_SLUG`.
### Step 3: Generate Plan Summary
Write a concise 2-3 sentence summary of the plan. This summary will be visible at the top of the PR description (above the collapsed full plan).
Guidelines:
- Focus on WHAT the plan does and WHY - Do not repeat the title - Plain text, no markdown headers or formatting - Avoid special shell characters (backticks, dollar signs) - Store as PLAN_SUMMARY
### Step 4: Run Save Command
Run this command with the session ID, branch slug, summary, and optional flags:
```bash erk exec plan-save --format json --session-id="027e57aa-eb20-41fe-a4d3-129b0d6cf008" --branch-slug="${BRANCH_SLUG}" --summary="${PLAN_SUMMARY}" ${PLAN_TYPE_FLAG} ${OBJECTIVE_FLAG} ```
Parse the JSON output to extract `plan_number` for verification in Step 5.
If the command fails, display the error and stop.
### Step 5: Verify Objective Link (if applicable)
**Only run this step if `objective_issue` is non-null in the JSON output from Step 4.**
Verify the objective link was saved correctly:
```bash erk exec get-plan-metadata <plan_number> objective_issue ```
Parse the JSON response:
- If `success: true` and `value` matches the expected objective number: verification passed - If `success: false` or value doesn't match: verification failed
**On verification success:**
Display: `Verified objective link: #<objective-number>`
**On verification failure:**
Display error and remediation steps:
``` ERROR: Objective link verification failed Expected objective: #<expected> Actual: <actual-or-null>
The plan was saved but without the correct objective link. Fix: Close draft PR #<plan_number>, ensure the objective-context marker exists, and re-run /erk:plan-save. ```
Exit without creating the plan-saved marker. The session continues so the user can retry.
### Step 6: Update Objective Roadmap (if objective linked)
**Only run this step if `objective_issue` was non-null in JSON output and verification passed.**
Update the objective's roadmap table to show that a plan has been created for this node:
1. **Read the roadmap node marker** to get the node ID:
```bash step_id=$(erk exec marker read --session-id "027e57aa-eb20-41fe-a4d3-129b0d6cf008" roadmap-step) ```
If the marker doesn't exist (command fails), skip this step - the plan wasn't created via `objective-plan`.
2. **Update the roadmap table** using the dedicated command:
```bash erk exec update-objective-node <objective-issue> --node "$step_id" --pr "#<plan_number>" --status in_progress ```
This atomically fetches the objective body, finds the matching node row, sets the Status cell to `in-progress`, and writes the updated body back.
3. **Report the update:**
Display: `Updated objective #<objective-issue> roadmap: node <step_id> → plan #<plan_number>`
**Error handling:** If the roadmap update fails, warn but continue - the plan was saved successfully, just the roadmap tracking didn't update. The user can manually update the objective.
### Step 7: Display Results
**If JSON contains `skipped_duplicate: true`:**
Display: `Plan already saved as #<plan_number> (duplicate skipped)`
If `branch_name` is present in the JSON, display the same next-steps block as the success case below.
If `branch_name` is absent, display only: `View PR: <plan_url>`
Return immediately (skip Steps 5, 6 above if not already executed).
**Otherwise, on success**, display:
``` Plan "<title>" saved as draft PR #<plan_number> URL: <issue_url> ```
**Slot options block:**
The "OR exit Claude Code first" section should show both slot allocation options, with the recommended one listed first based on trunk detection:
If **on trunk = true** (new-wt recommended → "In new wt" listed first):
``` OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of:
Implement plan #<plan_number>: In new wt: source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement (dangerously): source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement -d In current wt: source "$(erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement (dangerously): source "$(erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement -d
Checkout plan #<plan_number>: In new wt: erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> In current wt: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number>
Dispatch to queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
If **on trunk = false** (same slot recommended → "Here" listed first):
``` OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of:
Implement plan #<plan_number>: In current wt: source "$(erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement (dangerously): source "$(erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement -d In new wt: source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement (dangerously): source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement -d
Checkout plan #<plan_number>: In current wt: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> In new wt: erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number>
Dispatch to queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
**Next steps block:**
``` Next steps:
View PR: <plan_url>
In Claude Code: Dispatch to queue: /erk:pr-dispatch — Dispatch plan for remote agent implementation
OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of: Checkout plan #<plan_number>: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> Dispatch to queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
If objective was verified, also display: `Verified objective link: #<objective-number>`
If the JSON output contains `slot_name` and `slot_objective_updated: true`, also display: `Slot objective updated: <slot_name> → #<objective-number>`
**Note:** Slot objective updates are handled automatically by `plan-save` when an objective is linked via the session marker - no separate command call needed.
On failure, display the error message and suggest:
- Checking that a plan exists (enter Plan mode and exit it first) - Verifying GitHub CLI authentication (`gh auth status`) - Checking network connectivity
### Step 5: Push Planning Session
After saving successfully, push the planning session for cross-machine learning. This captures the in-progress session state so it's available even if implementation happens on a different machine.
Run `erk exec capture-session-info` to get the session file path, then push it:
```bash erk exec upload-impl-session --session-id "027e57aa-eb20-41fe-a4d3-129b0d6cf008" 2>/dev/null || true ```
If the plan was saved successfully and `plan_number` is known, use push-session directly for better stage tracking:
```bash erk exec push-session \ --session-file "<session_file_path>" \ --session-id "027e57aa-eb20-41fe-a4d3-129b0d6cf008" \ --stage planning \ --source local \ --plan-id <plan_number> \ 2>/dev/null || true ```
Where `<session_file_path>` is obtained from `erk exec capture-session-info` output and `<plan_number>` is from Step 2's JSON output.
**Note:** This is non-critical. If it fails, the plan was still saved successfully. The `2>/dev/null || true` ensures graceful degradation.
## Session Tracking
After successfully saving a plan, the plan number is stored in a marker file that enables automatic plan updates in the same session.
**To read the saved plan number:**
```bash erk exec marker read --session-id "027e57aa-eb20-41fe-a4d3-129b0d6cf008" plan-saved-issue ```
This returns the plan number (exit code 0) or exits with code 1 if no plan was saved in this session.
session.assistant:
Let me execute the plan-save steps.
**Branch slug:** `ci-setup-uv-caching`
**Plan summary:** Replaces actions/setup-python@v5 and pip-installed uv with astral-sh/setup-uv@v5 in the CI pipeline. This eliminates python.org download timeouts and adds built-in dependency caching, matching the approach already used in remote workflows.

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: erk exec plan-save --format json --session-id="027e57aa-eb20-41fe-a4d3-129b0d6cf008" --bra...[truncated 172 chars]...adds built-in dependency caching, matching the approach already used in remote workflows."

session.tool_result: {"success": true, "plan_number": 8722, "plan_url": "https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/pull/8722", "title": "[erk-plan] Plan: Switch CI Python setup to astral-sh/setup-uv", "branch_name": "plnd/ci-setup-uv-caching-03-04-0855", "plan_backend": "planned_pr", "objective_issue": null, "archived_to": "/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.erk/scratch/sessions/027e57aa-eb20-41fe-a4d3-129b0d6cf008/plan_snapshots/000001-a1a249b4"}

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: erk exec push-session \ --session-file "/Users/schrockn/.claude/projects/-Users-schroc...[truncated 94 chars]...\ --stage planning \ --source local \ --plan-id 8722 \ 2>/dev/null || true

session.assistant:
Plan "Switch CI Python setup to astral-sh/setup-uv" saved as draft PR #8722 URL: https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/pull/8722
**Next steps:**
View PR: https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/pull/8722
In Claude Code: Dispatch to queue: `/erk:pr-dispatch` — Dispatch plan for remote agent implementation
OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of:
Implement plan #8722: In new wt: `source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan 8722 --script)" && erk implement` (dangerously): `source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan 8722 --script)" && erk implement -d` In current wt: `source "$(erk br co --for-plan 8722 --script)" && erk implement` (dangerously): `source "$(erk br co --for-plan 8722 --script)" && erk implement -d`
Checkout plan #8722: In new wt: `erk br co --new-slot --for-plan 8722` In current wt: `erk br co --for-plan 8722`
Dispatch to queue: `erk pr dispatch 8722`
